evidence: The word 'black' strongly resonates with perception (direct sensory experience of darkness) and semantic meaning (core essence of absence). It also connects to material existence (physical darkness), symmetry as the destructive counterpart to light, and predictable patterns (daily cycles). Lower scores reflect its functional utility (e.g., rest) and aesthetic use (e.g., art).

Black resonates most strongly with its material presence (ontological.material), perceptual reality (epistemological.perception), and essential role as darkness's counterpart to light (resonance.essential). It strongly connects to cosmic/stellar scales, destructive symmetry, and aesthetic value. Predictable lawfulness reflects its consistent presence in darkness cycles.

The highest scores reflect 'black' (v) as a physical process (ontological.material), essential to darkness' core essence (resonance.essential), and its opposition to light (symmetry.destructive). Lower scores relate to individual-scale experiences (e.g., vision blacking out) and predictable patterns (e.g., nightfall).

The verb 'black out' strongly resonates with material reality (10) as it directly enacts darkness. Essential resonance (7) reflects its core essence as absence of light. Cyclical symmetry (5) and predictable lawfulness (6) tie to day-night cycles. Aesthetic value (3) arises from its role in phenomena like starry skies.

The term 'coal black' strongly resonates with material existence (ontological.material) and the essential/existential nature of darkness (resonance.essential/existential). Its sensory perception (epistemological.perception) and aesthetic value (value_generation.aesthetic) are central to its use as a vivid descriptor of darkness. Scores for permanence and predictability reflect coal's enduring, consistent darkness.

The word 'dark' strongly resonates with material and perceptual aspects (physical absence of light, direct sensory experience). Its opposition to light (symmetry.destructive) is central. Resonance at essential/existential levels reflects symbolic depth (e.g., mystery, void). Scale scores reflect its presence from cosmic (universal darkness) to stellar (planetary night), though most contexts are lower-level.
